metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete mechanism for distinguishing infra-failure from test-failure (e.g., 'job name matches pattern `infra-.*`', 'Verified vote message contains `INFRA_FAILURE`', or 'retry ALL Verified -1 on first occurrence and only skip retry when the vote message contains a known test-failure marker') so the executor can implement the conditional without investigation.
findings.finding: The CI-flake AC conditions retry behavior on whether 'the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure' but never specifies the distinguishing mechanism — which Gerrit label value, which CI job name pattern, or which API field signals infra-failure vs. test-failure. An executor cannot implement this conditional without investigation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck bullet: 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures.'
findings.suggested_fix: State a default: 'If the CI system does not provide a structured infra-vs-test signal, retry ALL Verified -1 once (the 1-retry bound still limits blast radius). If the Gerrit vote message contains a bot-posted structured tag such as `INFRA_FAILURE`, limit retry to those.' This turns the open conditional into an actionable decision with a safe default.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete machine-readable signal that distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure (e.g., always retry unconditionally since the signal doesn't exist yet, or defer the distinction to a later story with a TODO comment), rather than leaving the condition unimplementable; if the distinction is aspirational, simplify AC1 to 'retry unconditionally on the same patchset once before handing back'.
findings.finding: The AC1 conditional 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' assumes a machine-readable failure-type signal that does not exist in the current Gerrit/CI integration. The actual gerrit-verify workflow casts a single undifferentiated `Verified -1` for all failure modes (test failures, infra errors, network timeouts). The vote message carries only a run URL, not a structured failure-type tag readable from the Gerrit REST API. There is no specified mechanism (label value, vote message parsing rule, or secondary signal) by which the bot could distinguish infra from test failure, making this branch of the retry logic unimplementable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (AC1): 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the conditional with unconditional single retry: 'on a post-rebase Verified -1, issue one auto-recheck on the same patchset before recording ci_failed and stripping Autosubmit.' The infra/test distinction can be added in a follow-on story once the CI infrastructure exposes a structured failure-type signal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a testing plan covering: (a) happy-path and failure/retry-boundary tests for the CI-flake auto-recheck (1-retry cap, infra vs test-failure discrimination, recheck-error handling), (b) 409 rebase-conflict routing with a verified absence of `allow_conflicts`, (c) whole-stack Autosubmit removal including 404/409 no-op, (d) restart/idempotency recovery for unacknowledged markers, and (e) at least one live/integration acceptance criterion exercising the Gerrit API boundary for the 409 and label-removal paths so the suite cannot go green with mocks alone.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new user-facing failure-handling flows (CI-fail auto-recheck, 409 rebase-conflict hand-back, idempotent restart recovery, Autosubmit label removal with 404/409 no-op) but contains zero testing content anywhere — no unit tests, integration tests, test scenarios, or acceptance-test strategy are described for any of these paths, including the failure/timeout/edge paths the plan itself centers on.
findings.location: Entire plan — Acceptance Criteria section, all ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Testing' section to the plan with: unit tests for the retry-count boundary (exactly 1 retry), a contract test asserting `allow_conflicts` is never set on a rebase call, a table-driven test for label-removal idempotency (200/404/409 responses), a simulated-restart test verifying an `acknowledged=false` marker re-drives removal, and an integration/e2e acceptance criterion that fires a real (or real-shaped) Gerrit 409 response and asserts full-stack Autosubmit removal — this prevents proxy-validation from hiding live-path breakage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the AC1 infra-failure/test-failure conditional: either drop it (retry all Verified -1 once regardless of cause, which the binary vote surface supports) or specify the exact signal source (e.g., a GitHub Actions jobs API call) and the authentication/scope it requires.
findings.finding: The AC1 conditional 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' introduces conditional behavior gated on a signal distinction that does not exist in the current CI vote surface: gerrit-verify.yaml casts a flat binary Verified +1/-1 (via im-open/workflow-conclusion → WORKFLOW_CONCLUSION), with no infra-vs-test annotation on the Gerrit label. The condition is either permanently false (making it dead code) or requires an unspecified GitHub Actions API call to read run-level job conclusions — which is undescribed scope.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back), AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' with 'Retry once on any Verified -1 on the same patchset; the binary vote surface provides no infra/test distinction.' This matches what the CI system actually exposes and preserves the zero-babysitting goal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command or integration test reference for each AC (e.g., for CI-fail: 'simulate Verified -1 twice on a test change and assert Autosubmit label absent on all stack members via `ssh gerrit query --format=JSON change:<id> --current-patch-set | jq -e ".currentPatchSet.approvals[] | select(.type=="Autosubmit")" exits non-zero`; for needs_rebase: assert marker file exists at expected path with correct SHA).
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion has a concrete proving command — the plan contains zero test commands, integration checks, or deterministic verification steps. Every AC is a behavioral statement with no 'how to verify done' anchor, violating E6(a) and E6(c).
findings.location: All 7 Acceptance Criteria bullets

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before build-out: spike the target CI system's Verified vote payload to confirm whether infra-failure vs test-failure is distinguishable via a label message, vote sub-type, or separate vote; document the signal (or its absence) and derive the retry count from historical flake data or a stated policy rationale.
findings.finding: The plan asserts "Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures" without verifying that the actual CI/Gerrit integration provides a machine-readable distinction between infra and test failures. If the CI system emits only a single Verified -1 vote with no sub-type signal, the conditional is silently a no-op (always retry, or never filter), breaking the zero-babysitting goal unpredictably. The 1-retry bound is also a threshold asserted without any measurement of actual CI flake rates to justify it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-implementation spike: query recent Gerrit Verified -1 events in the target project, inspect the vote message/metadata format, and check whether the CI system sets distinguishable fields. Record the outcome in the plan; if no distinction is available, simplify the AC to "always retry once on Verified -1" and add a TODO to revisit when the CI system is upgraded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the observable CI signal used to distinguish infra-failure from test-failure (e.g., a specific Gerrit label value, CI job name pattern, or message keyword), and state the fallback behaviour (retry or skip retry) when the distinction cannot be made.
findings.finding: The AC specifies 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures', but neither the plan body nor the AC provides an observable mechanism for HOW this distinction is made (e.g., which Gerrit vote label, CI job field, or message pattern signals infra vs test failure), nor what the fallback behaviour is when the distinction is indistinguishable. This leaves a non-deferred goal without an end-state-observable proof.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck bullet: 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-bullet under the CI-flake AC: 'Infra-failure is identified by [specific signal, e.g., a `Verified -1` vote whose message matches /infrastructure|timeout|quota/i, or a designated CI job category]. When the signal is absent or ambiguous, default to [retry once / do not retry] to avoid false positives.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether the existing `Verified` label / `gerrit-verify.yaml` pipeline exposes a machine-readable infra-failure-vs-test-failure distinction; if it does not, either remove the conditional (retry all single `Verified -1` flakes once) or specify the mechanism (e.g., a new structured vote message field, a separate label value, or a GitHub Actions job-conclusion endpoint call) that the autolander will use to discriminate.
findings.finding: The CI-flake auto-recheck AC asserts conditional behavior 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' — but the actual CI integration (`gerrit-verify.yaml` + `im-open/workflow-conclusion`) exposes only a single binary `WORKFLOW_CONCLUSION` (success/failure/cancelled) value, and the `Verified` label is a single -1..0..+1 integer with no sub-channel for failure category. No mechanism in the current codebase allows the autolander to distinguish an infra-failure `Verified -1` from a test-failure `Verified -1`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either specify the observable mechanism by which the autolander distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure (e.g. GitHub Actions REST `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions/runs/{run_id}/jobs` job-level conclusion, requiring a `GITHUB_TOKEN` with `actions:read` scope provisioned for the bot), or simplify AC1 to always retry once on any `Verified -1` without the infra/test distinction.
findings.finding: AC1 conditions selective retry on 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' — but the actual CI system (gerrit-verify.yaml) casts only a binary `Verified +1` or `Verified -1` label (from `WORKFLOW_CONCLUSION`). No structured infra-vs-test failure sub-type is conveyed via the Gerrit label or any machine-readable side channel back to the autolander. The plan asserts a conditional capability that the current CI-to-Gerrit integration does not provide, making the infra-failure branch of AC1 unimplementable without additional work.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the machine-readable mechanism by which the autolander distinguishes CI infra-failure from test-failure (e.g., specific Verified vote message pattern, GitHub Actions conclusion field, or explicit acknowledgment that no distinction is currently possible and all Verified -1 retries are unconditional).
findings.finding: The CI-flake AC specifies conditional retry logic — 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' — but neither the plan nor any referenced schema/enum defines a machine-readable mechanism for making this distinction. The actual Verified label (verified in infra/gerrit/project.config and infra/runbooks/two-vote-gate-rollback.md) is a scalar -1/0/+1 with an optional freetext message URL; there is no structured failure-type field, no enum, and no API surface that exposes infra-failure vs test-failure to a consumer.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) specify that the current CI integration casts a single scalar Verified -1 with no structured failure-type, so the retry is unconditional (simpler, matches actual capability), or (b) define an explicit enum/schema field (e.g., parse the GitHub Actions `conclusion` field via the URL in the Verified vote message) and co-locate the parsing protocol with the marker schema.

findings.finding: The AC-1 conditional 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' references a signal/vocabulary that is never defined anywhere in the plan. The gerrit-verify.yaml workflow casts a single integer Verified vote (-1/0/+1) with no sub-type field; adapter.py's _TAG_SUFFIXES coverage-gap sub-reasons apply only to LLM-Review message bodies, not to Verified. The plan provides no enum of infra-failure indicators (exit code, job name pattern, message substring, or separate Gerrit label), no decision rule for classifying a -1 as infra vs test, and no fallback behavior for the common case when the signal is absent or indistinguishable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (AC-1)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) specify that the bot inspects the GitHub Actions run conclusion for each job by calling `GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions/runs?...` and classifying any run where only infrastructure jobs (e.g., matching `runner-setup`, `clear-vote`) failed as infra-failure, with a defined pattern list; or (b) simplify to 'always retry once on any Verified -1' and remove the conditional, since the infra/test distinction cannot be reliably read from the Gerrit vote alone.

findings.finding: The AC-1 conditional 'where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' references a signal that does not exist in the current gerrit-verify.yaml pipeline: all CI failures cast an undifferentiated `Verified -1` via a single `WORKFLOW_CONCLUSION` env var with no structured infra/test tag. The conditional branch that would 'only retry infra-failures' is permanently vacuous today — making it a YAGNI hypothetical serving a future CI signal, not the current done-definition.
findings.location: AC: CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Where the CI signal distinguishes infra-failure from test-failure, only retry infra-failures' with 'retry once on any `Verified -1`' — matching the actual undifferentiated signal. If infra-tagging is planned, capture it as a separate future story rather than a conditional that silently does nothing today.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or replace the 'S9 (batch failure)' consumer reference in the Outcome record section — S9 is archived per the parent epic; update the consumer list to only name live stories (e.g., S4 land command), and note that any future Phase-2 batching story will define its own schema contract when promoted from idea c3a9.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'S9 (batch failure)' as a named live consumer of the S3 marker cache and state-dir schema, but S9 is explicitly archived in the parent epic (f1fa-57d8-07cb-44af), which states 'stories S7-S9 archived' and 'cross-stack batching was evaluated by a design spike and rejected.' The S9 consumer reference is an unverifiable/false assertion that leaves the marker schema's actual downstream consumer set undefined.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache + live Gerrit state as the outcome.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache + live Gerrit state as the outcome.' with 'S4 (land command) consumes this cache + live Gerrit state as the outcome. (S9 batch failure is archived — idea c3a9-5f4c-bdc6-4e27 is the deferred Phase-2 throughput follow-on and will define its own schema contract if promoted.)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific Gerrit REST endpoint and required capability the bot uses to post the `recheck` comment (e.g. `POST /a/changes/{change-id}/revisions/{revision}/review` with `{"message": "recheck"}`), and confirm the bot's service account has the `Reply on Behalf of Others` or equivalent capability to post comments.
findings.finding: The plan's AC1 mandates a 'bounded auto-recheck — 1 retry on the SAME patchset' but never names the Gerrit REST endpoint or mechanism by which the bot triggers CI re-execution. In this repo, CI re-dispatch works by posting a Gerrit comment containing the text 'recheck' (confirmed in gerrit_to_platform.ini.template: `[mapping "comment-added"] recheck = verify`), which g2p intercepts and dispatches gerrit-verify.yaml. The bot would need to call a Gerrit REST endpoint (e.g. `POST /a/changes/{change-id}/revisions/{revision}/review` with `{"message": "recheck"}`) — a new, unspecified API surface not present in the current autolander helper.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'The bot triggers the recheck by calling `POST /a/changes/{change-id}/revisions/{revision}/review` with body `{"message": "recheck"}` (the g2p `comment-added` hook re-dispatches CI on this text); the bot's service account must hold the `Reply` Gerrit capability.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies removing 'Autosubmit from all changes in the affected stack' but never names the concrete Gerrit REST API endpoint or request body for label removal. The standard mechanism is `POST /changes/{id}/revisions/current/review` with `{"labels": {"Autosubmit": 0}}` — but 'current' may reference the wrong patchset if the change has advanced, and voting as the bot may require the bot to have previously voted +1 (Gerrit only allows withdrawing your own vote). Auth scope for this action is also unspecified in S3.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-fail AC (second bullet) and Rebase-conflict AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the recheck invocation mechanism in the CI-flake AC: state that the bot should POST a Gerrit comment containing 'recheck' (via `POST /changes/{id}/revisions/current/review` or the comments endpoint) which g2p maps to re-dispatching gerrit-verify.yaml, matching the existing repo recheck pattern.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'do a bounded auto-recheck' as a concrete step but never defines the mechanism for triggering the recheck. The repo's actual recheck implementation is a Gerrit comment containing the literal string 'recheck' which g2p maps to the 'verify' workflow (confirmed in infra/gerrit/gerrit_to_platform.ini.template line 43: 'recheck = verify'). No Gerrit REST API endpoint, comment text, or alternative invocation method is named in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the CI-flake AC: 'Trigger recheck by posting a Gerrit comment "recheck" via POST /changes/{id}/revisions/current/review (the existing g2p comment-added → verify mapping handles dispatch; see infra/gerrit/gerrit_to_platform.ini.template).'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the termination policy for the restart-driven Autosubmit-removal loop: either (a) specify which non-404/409 HTTP errors are treated as no-op (e.g., all 4xx), (b) add a max-retry count after which the marker is force-acknowledged with an error log, or (c) explicitly state 'retry indefinitely with exponential back-off — operator must resolve Gerrit connectivity'.
findings.finding: The idempotency/restart AC ('on restart, any acknowledged=false marker re-drives the removal to completion') specifies no terminal failure condition or retry limit for Autosubmit removal — the plan states label-removal treats 404/409 as no-op but does not address permanent API errors or network failures, leaving the restart loop's termination undefined.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'on restart, any `acknowledged=false` marker re-drives the removal to completion (idempotent)'; AC: 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Non-404/409 errors during Autosubmit removal are retried with exponential back-off (max 5 attempts, base 30 s); after exhausting retries the marker is marked `acknowledged=error` and a log line is emitted — the stack is not re-driven until the operator clears the marker.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Collapse the 'Persisted state' AC into a reference to the 'Outcome record' section and add only the delta testable predicate (e.g., 'a marker with a stale SHA is silently skipped and does not trigger Autosubmit-removal'), removing the verbatim restatement of the body.
findings.finding: The 'Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache' AC is a near-duplicate of the 'Outcome record' section: both specify that only `needs_rebase` is persisted, that markers are keyed by `change_id + patchset_SHA`, that stale-SHA markers are ignored, that `merged`/`ci_failed`/`review_failed` are never persisted, and that a rolling log line is kept. The AC adds no new testable verification beyond restating the body prose.
findings.location: AC 'Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache' vs 'Outcome record' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify retry-with-backoff (e.g., exponential, max 3 attempts) for all Gerrit API calls (label removal, POST /rebase, recheck trigger), and define how non-404/409 error codes are surfaced vs. retried.
findings.finding: Transient Gerrit API errors (5xx, network timeouts) on Autosubmit label removal and POST /rebase are not addressed. The plan specifies 404/409 as no-ops but is silent on other error codes, leaving error states potentially swallowed or causing unhandled exceptions.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Idempotent ordering under retries/restart AC; CI-fail AC; Rebase-conflict AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a RETIRE path for `needs_rebase` markers: either explicit deletion when acknowledged=true and the change is merged/abandoned, or a TTL-based compaction sweep that removes markers older than N days.
findings.finding: There is no RETIRE/cleanup path for stale `needs_rebase` marker files in the state dir. The plan specifies markers are IGNORED when the patchset SHA no longer matches, but the files are never deleted, causing unbounded accumulation over time.
findings.location: Outcome record section — marker shape and self-invalidating description
findings.suggested_fix: On each bot run, after processing a stack, delete any marker for that stack where acknowledged=true OR where the change is no longer open in Gerrit. Alternatively, add a periodic compaction job that deletes markers older than 30 days.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `recheck_issued` boolean (or `recheck_issued_at` timestamp) to the needs_rebase/ci-tracking marker shape — or persist a minimal ci_recheck_pending marker keyed by change_id+patchset_SHA — so the 1-retry bound survives a bot restart and the idempotency AC covers recheck re-drive.
findings.finding: The 'bounded auto-recheck — 1 retry on the SAME patchset' constraint has no specified durable storage for the retry counter. The needs_rebase marker shape is fully specified, but ci_failed is derived live (never persisted). The retry count for the ci-flake recheck is implicitly in-memory only. If the bot restarts after posting the recheck comment but before observing the second Verified result, the in-memory counter is lost and the bot will issue a second recheck on restart, violating the 1-retry bound. The idempotency AC mentions acknowledged-flag re-driving for Autosubmit removal but is silent on recheck-counter durability.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (AC1) and Idempotent ordering under retries/restart (AC7)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback schema-state assertion: specify what happens to existing `needs_rebase` marker files if S3 is rolled back (e.g., old-code readers treat unknown fields as absent and the self-invalidating SHA property is sufficient, OR a cleanup/migration step removes markers with unrecognized shapes), and confirm no `Autosubmit`-removal re-drive is silently lost on rollback.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema STATE of the state directory after a rollback of the S3 deployment. If S3 is deployed, writes `needs_rebase` markers (with the new `acknowledged`, `stack_id`, `change_ids[]`, etc. fields), and is then rolled back to pre-S3 code, the stale marker files remain in the state dir. Pre-S3 consumers (S4, S9) would encounter files in an unknown shape. The plan's self-invalidating SHA property partially mitigates this but does not constitute a full post-rollback schema-state assertion as required by T11.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC 'Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache'; AC 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief rollback section stating: (1) old-code readers MUST treat unknown marker fields (e.g., `acknowledged`) as absent and continue to self-validate by SHA — verify this is true of S4/S9 consumers; (2) any marker with `acknowledged=false` that survives a rollback will be re-driven to completion when S3 is re-deployed (since the re-drive is idempotent); (3) if old-code consumers are NOT forward-compatible with new marker fields, provide a compensating-forward step (e.g., a one-time scan that strips `Autosubmit` from all changes whose marker is `acknowledged=false`) rather than assuming reversibility.

findings.finding: The plan introduces a new persisted marker format (`needs_rebase` markers with `acknowledged` flag in the project-infra state dir) but makes no assertion about the schema state after a deployment rollback. If a rollback occurs after markers have been written (especially with `acknowledged=false`), a prior version of the bot that does not understand the marker schema will encounter unknown files or misparse them, potentially silently skipping re-drive of Autosubmit removal — leaving stacks permanently stuck with Autosubmit stripped but no agent notification. The plan states only forward-operation semantics.
findings.location: Outcome record section; AC: 'Idempotent ordering under retries/restart'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `schema_version` field to the marker shape (e.g., `schema_version: 1`). Specify that older bot versions encountering an unrecognised `schema_version` emit a warning and skip (rather than silently ignore) the marker, and that a rollback runbook step includes either draining acknowledged=false markers before rollback or running a cleanup script that sets acknowledged=true on any marker whose Gerrit state confirms Autosubmit is already absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'What' section's 'single source of truth' claim with the 'Outcome record' section's 'Gerrit is the source of truth / cache is a reconstruct' framing — either reword 'What' to say 'the recorded outcome (derived live from Gerrit + the needs_rebase cache) is the contract signal' or add a clarifying parenthetical that 'single source of truth' refers to the live-Gerrit-derived outcome concept, not the persisted marker.
findings.finding: The 'What' section calls 'the recorded outcome' 'the single source of truth consumed by the agent contract', but the 'Outcome record' section immediately contradicts this by declaring 'Gerrit is the source of truth; the persisted store is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache, not an authoritative record.' An implementer reading both sections gets two conflicting answers about which system is authoritative.
findings.location: 'What' section (last sentence) vs 'Outcome record' section (first sentence)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, replace 'The recorded outcome is the single source of truth consumed by the agent contract' with 'The live-Gerrit-derived outcome (augmented by the needs_rebase cache) is the single signal consumed by the agent contract — the persisted store is a cache, not an authority.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the Idempotent ordering AC) to specify which marker fields are required vs optional and under what conditions — at minimum, assert that `failing_change_id` is populated on CI-fail, `tested_sha` captures the patchset SHA under test, and `recorded_at` is an ISO timestamp.
findings.finding: The marker shape defined in 'Outcome record' (`failing_change_id?`, `tested_sha`, `recorded_at`) has no corresponding acceptance criterion that verifies these fields are present with correct values. They are specified deliverables with no covering criterion.
findings.location: 'Outcome record' section — marker shape definition; no matching AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the recheck retry limit into a named, documented constant or configuration value (e.g. `AUTO_RECHECK_MAX_RETRIES`) so it can be tuned without a code change.
findings.finding: The hardcoded retry count of 1 is a policy threshold expected to evolve with CI infrastructure changes, but the plan makes no provision for it to be configurable. CI flakiness rates change over time and differ across projects; a hardcoded constant will require a code change to tune.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)
findings.suggested_fix: Define `AUTO_RECHECK_MAX_RETRIES = 1` as a named constant in a central config/constants module and reference it from the recheck logic; document the intent and expected range in a comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the Gerrit-as-source-of-truth / reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache architecture in a durable design document (ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or equivalent) and reference it from the S3 plan and any consuming stories (S4, S9).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — Gerrit as authoritative source of truth with a self-invalidating reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache for `needs_rebase` markers — that spans multiple stories (S2/S3/S4/S9) and inverts the typical bot-state model. No ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc capture is called for anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Outcome record section; Acceptance Criteria — Persisted state is a reconstruct-from-Gerrit cache

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a maximum wait duration (e.g., N minutes) for the post-recheck CI result; on timeout, treat the outcome as `ci_failed` and proceed to Autosubmit removal rather than blocking indefinitely.
findings.finding: No timeout is specified for waiting on the CI result after the auto-recheck trigger. The plan bounds the retry count (1 recheck) but not the maximum wait duration; if CI hangs or its event never arrives, the bot stalls indefinitely on that stack, blocking the loop.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — CI-flake auto-recheck AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'If no new Verified vote arrives within T minutes of the recheck trigger, treat the stack as ci_failed and proceed to Autosubmit removal.' T should be derived from the project's typical CI turnaround (e.g., 2× median CI duration).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at minimum a structured metric/counter (or named log event readable by the monitoring stack) for each outcome type (ci_failed, needs_rebase, autosubmit_removal_error) so operators can observe failure rates and alert on anomalies.
findings.finding: Observability for new failure points is underspecified. The plan mentions only 'a rolling per-transition log line for debugging' but no metrics, structured events, or alerts for the failure modes this story introduces (CI failures, rebase conflicts, Gerrit API errors during label removal).
findings.location: Outcome record section; Acceptance Criteria — Persisted state AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that marker file writes use an atomic replace (write to a tmp file in the same directory, then rename/mv) and document the concurrency model (e.g., single-writer process, or advisory lock) to prevent TOCTOU races.
findings.finding: Concurrent writes to marker files are not specified as atomic. If two bot instances (e.g., a restart overlapping with the previous instance) both detect the same conflict and write the marker, or if one reads acknowledged=false and another writes acknowledged=true simultaneously, there is a TOCTOU race with no locking or atomic swap described.
findings.location: Outcome record section — 'A re-run overwrites the prior marker'; Acceptance Criteria — Idempotent ordering AC
findings.suggested_fix: Write marker to `<key>.tmp`, then `rename()` to `<key>` (atomic on POSIX). If multiple-writer is possible, use an advisory lock file or a DB with atomic upsert (e.g., SQLite with WAL mode).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate S4 and S9 as named downstream consumers of the S3 marker schema by adding their ticket IDs (and expected file paths once known) to the plan, so the state-dir schema contract is explicitly tracked across stories.
findings.finding: S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) are named as consumers of the S3-produced marker/state-dir and marker schema, but neither their file paths nor their ticket IDs are enumerated anywhere in the plan. If the marker shape or state-dir path changes, the plan gives no mechanism for an executor to locate and update these callers.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'S4 (land command) and S9 (batch failure) consume this cache + live Gerrit state as the outcome.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Outcome record section, add: 'Consumers: S4 (`<ticket-id>`, `infra/autolander/land.py` or equivalent) and S9 (`<ticket-id>`, `infra/autolander/batch.py` or equivalent) — these stories must be updated if the marker shape changes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete filesystem path for the auto-lander state directory (e.g. `/var/lib/autolander/state/` or a named Docker volume path) so S3's write target, S5's volume mount, and S4/S9's read path are all consistent.
findings.finding: The 'project-infra state dir' where `needs_rebase` markers are persisted is never given a concrete filesystem path. S3 writes to it, S4/S9 read from it, and the S5 deploy story must mount/provision it — but no path (e.g. `/var/lib/autolander/state/` or a volume name) is specified.
findings.location: Outcome record section: 'persist ONLY `needs_rebase` markers in the auto-lander state dir (project-infra state, NOT a rebar event)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the restart re-drive protocol with the marker schema: explicitly state that on acknowledged=false restart, the bot iterates ALL change_ids[] in the marker and attempts Autosubmit removal for each (treating 404 as already-removed/success), rather than leaving this rule only in the idempotency AC.
findings.finding: The marker schema includes a single boolean `acknowledged` flag to track completion of Autosubmit-removal across all stack members, but the schema has no per-change completion tracking. The restart re-drive protocol ('on restart, any acknowledged=false marker re-drives the removal to completion') cannot determine which specific changes in `change_ids[]` already had Autosubmit removed vs. which still need it, without querying Gerrit live. This dependency on live Gerrit state for restart reconciliation is implicit and not co-located with the schema definition as a processing protocol.
findings.location: Outcome record section; Acceptance Criteria — Idempotent ordering under retries/restart

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the stack_id fallback rule to cover multi-change stacks without a Gerrit topic (e.g., 'for a multi-change stack with no topic, fall back to the tip change-id' or 'assign a synthetic topic at selection time').
findings.finding: The plan defines stack_id as 'the Gerrit topic the auto-lander assigns to a stack at selection time (falling back to the tip change-id for a single-change stack)' but does not specify the stack_id value for the case where a stack has multiple related changes (detected via RelatedChanges per S2a) but no Gerrit topic is set. This is a decision rule gap in the schema's key derivation logic.
findings.location: Outcome record section — stack_id definition
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'For a multi-change stack detected via RelatedChanges but with no Gerrit topic, use the tip change-id as stack_id (same fallback as single-change).' Or specify that S2 always assigns a synthetic topic at selection time, making the no-topic case impossible.

findings.finding: The auto-recheck mechanism requires posting a `recheck` comment on the Gerrit change (the only available trigger: `gerrit_to_platform.ini.template` maps `comment-added: recheck = verify` so a `recheck` comment re-dispatches gerrit-verify.yaml). The CI-fail AC simultaneously states 'post NO bot comment.' These two constraints directly contradict each other — an implementer cannot trigger the recheck without violating the no-comment constraint, or satisfy the no-comment constraint without omitting the recheck entirely.
findings.location: AC — 'CI-flake auto-recheck (before hand-back)' and AC — 'CI-fail: … post NO bot comment'
findings.suggested_fix: In the CI-fail AC, change 'post NO bot comment' to 'post NO bot hand-back comment; the auto-recheck trigger (a system `recheck` comment on the failing change) is permitted and is not considered a redundant notification channel.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
